title: Needle-felted hare
subtitle: A sitting brown hare in natural undyed fleece
excerpt: A sitting hare approximately 10 cm tall built from natural brown Jacob or Shetland fleece, with long ears supported by fine wire so they stand upright. Simple two-piece construction makes this a good first needle-felted animal. No dyeing required.
sourceNotes: Synthesised from beginner needle-felted animal patterns in common UK guild teaching. Two-piece body-and-head construction with wire ears is the standard first-animal method. Primary references: guild teaching notes; no single public domain text.

glossaryTerms.definition: Wool fibre drawn into a long, loosely twisted ribbon, ready for spinning or felting.
Combed wool fibres aligned in parallel. Smoother and finer than roving; good for surface detail and colour work.
Fleece from Jacob sheep, naturally occurring in black and white. Produces speckled or blended tones without dyeing.
The tiny notch cut into a felting needle shaft. Barbs catch wool fibres and push them together with each stabbing stroke.

content.text: Building a sitting hare is one of the best first needle-felted animal projects because the body shape is simple, the proportions are forgiving, and natural undyed
Jacob fleece

gives convincing colour without any dye work. Shetland moorit fleece is an equally good choice: both produce the warm brown-fawn tones of a wild hare. You will need a short length of fine wire only for the ears; the body is solid felted form, no armature.

attrs.title: Needle safety
attrs.body: Always stab straight in and out at 90 degrees. Enter foam, not a hard surface, on every stroke.

content.text: Main body: 50 g natural brown Jacob or Shetland moorit
roving
. Detail colours: a small amount of cream or off-white
wool tops

for the inner ears and chest, plus a tiny amount of black wool tops for the nose and eyes. Ears: two 6 cm lengths of 0.5 mm wire. Eyes: two 4 mm black plastic safety eyes (optional). You will also need craft pliers.
Method
Body. Take about 35 g of brown
roving

and roll it into a firm oval roughly 7 cm tall and 4 cm wide. This is a sitting body, so it should be slightly taller than it is wide. Place on your foam pad and stab all over with a 38-gauge needle, rotating constantly. Keep working until the oval holds its shape under gentle squeezing. The base of the body should be flat so the hare sits without rocking.

Head. Roll about 10 g of brown roving into a compact ball approximately 3 cm across. Felt separately until quite firm. A hare's head is narrower than a rabbit's: once your ball is firm, felt along the sides with two or three strokes to slightly elongate the shape. Press the muzzle end slightly forward and felt that area a little more to build the characteristic protruding nose.

Attaching head to body. Hold the head against the top of the body and check the proportions. The head should be roughly one-third the size of the body. Once satisfied, place the head on the body and felt through the join from multiple angles, including from the back, front, and each side, until the join disappears into the surface.

Ears. Bend each 6 cm wire in half to make a hairpin shape 3 cm tall. Bend the cut ends back 5 mm with pliers. Wrap each wire loop tightly in brown roving, working down from the tip. Felt the wrapped wire flat on the foam pad until you have a thin flat ear panel about 2.5 cm long. Lay a thin wisp of cream or off-white
wool tops

down the inner surface and felt in lightly so it blends at the edges. Push the base of each wire ear down into the top of the head and felt in firmly all around the base until the ear stands upright without wobbling.

Front legs. Roll two short sausages of brown roving, each about 3 cm long. Felt each to medium firmness, then attach to the front lower section of the body, angled slightly forward to give the hare its characteristic sitting-up posture. Felt in firmly at the join.
Facial detail. Felt a small area of cream wool tops over the chin and lower muzzle. Add a small black
wool tops

dot for the nose at the tip of the muzzle using a 42-gauge needle. If using safety eyes, push the posts through from outside and press the washers on from behind. If using felted eyes, felt two small dark dots with a 42-gauge needle, then add a tiny white highlight to each.
Finishing. Trim stray fibres with sharp scissors. Check the
barbs
on your needle: if it is dragging rather than sinking cleanly, replace it before doing the fine detail steps.

items.symptom: Ears flop to one side after attachment
items.cause: The wire base was not pushed deep enough into the head, or the join was not felted firmly enough
items.fix: Push the ear base deeper with a blunt darning needle, then felt all around the base in tight circles with a 38-gauge needle until the ear holds its position.

items.symptom: Body rocks and will not sit flat
items.cause: The base was not flattened during core building
items.fix: Place the body on a flat surface and press down firmly while felting several strokes straight down through the base until it flattens.

items.cause: Only one tone of roving was used
items.fix: Add thin wisps of a slightly lighter brown or fawn roving to the back and top of the head, felting in lightly so the tones blend naturally.

content.text: Display and care
The sitting hare needs no mounting: it rests on any flat surface. Natural undyed
Jacob fleece

and Shetland are more lightfast than dyed wools, but the piece will still fade slightly in direct sunlight over time. Dust with a soft brush rather than washing. Store in a box with cedar to deter moths.
